era_context: Late Bronze Age. Egypt under the 18th Dynasty was consolidating imperial power in the Levant and Nubia. The Hittite Empire was in its New Kingdom expansion phase. Mycenaean Greece was in Late Helladic II, with palace centers at Mycenae, Tiryns, and Pylos flourishing. Kassite Babylonia was stable. This era was characterized by interstate diplomacy, military competition, and the development of complex trade networks. Writing systems (Egyptian hieroglyphics, Hittite cuneiform, Linear B in Greece) were well-established.
disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are attested for 1512 BCE in any surviving source. The absence of documented events for this year is not itself disconfirming evidence; rather, it reflects the sparse documentation of the Late Bronze Age. Any claim about specific events in 1512 BCE would be speculative.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1512 BCE is essentially non-existent. No contemporary sources document specific events in this year. The era context is known from broader sources (temple inscriptions, clay tablets, archaeological evidence), but these are not dated to individual years. This year exemplifies the fundamental limitation of ancient history: for most years in antiquity, no documented events survive. The surviving sources are heavily biased toward Egypt and the Hittite Empire; other regions are archaeologically invisible or have left no written records. Chronological uncertainty is extreme; the assignment of this year to the Late Bronze Age is based on conventional chronology, which remains disputed among scholars.
